memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Complete chapter listing for The Mayor of Casterbridge'

memes.label: Abel Whittle
memes.description: A chronically tardy labourer in Henchard's employ whose mistreatment by Henchard and kindness from Farfrae crystallize the difference between the two men's characters.

memes.label: Alderman Tubber
memes.description: A "jocular" alderman of Casterbridge who appears at the council proceedings and the town's public occasions.

memes.label: Alderman Vatt
memes.description: An alderman of Casterbridge and neighbour who announces the death of Dr. Chalkfield to the town council.

memes.label: Ambition and Will
memes.description: The powerful ambition and indomitable will that drive Michael Henchard's meteoric rise — the same qualities that, unchecked by wisdom or temperance, become the instruments of his destruction.

memes.label: Appearance and Reality
memes.description: The persistent gap between public presentation and private truth — a central thematic concern of The Mayor of Casterbridge, expressed through secrets, disguises, reputations, and the town's relentless exposure of hidden truths. Showcase page for concept type.

memes.label: Budmouth
memes.description: The seaside resort town (modelled on Weymouth) that serves as a symbolic counterpoint to Casterbridge — a place of modern leisure, escape, and death. Showcase page for place type.

memes.label: Casterbridge
memes.description: The fictional Wessex town that serves as the primary setting of The Mayor of Casterbridge — a market-town modelled on Dorchester whose rhythms of commerce, class, and gossip shape the novel's action. Showcase page for place type.

memes.label: Character as Fate
memes.description: The novel's governing philosophical premise — that a person's character, not external circumstance or divine will, determines their destiny. This concept underpins Henchard's entire tragic arc and is Hardy's key innovation within the tragic tradition. Showcase page for concept type.

memes.label: Character Index
memes.description: A complete index of all characters in Thomas Hardy's The Mayor of Casterbridge, dynamically generated from wiki entries.

memes.label: Charl
memes.description: A deposed gamekeeper of Casterbridge, associated with Peter's Finger, who appears during the investigation of the skimmington-ride — one of Hardy's lower-class characters whose rough speech and marginal position contrast with the town's official civic life.

memes.label: Christopher Coney
memes.description: An elderly Casterbridge resident who represents the old ways — a traditionalist whose nostalgia for the past provides a counterpoint to the novel's theme of modernisation and change.

memes.label: Class and Social Order
memes.description: The rigid yet permeable class structure of rural Wessex — the social hierarchy that Henchard climbs and falls from, and the class anxieties that drive much of the novel's interpersonal drama.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: A complete index of all themes, motifs, and literary concepts in Thomas Hardy's The Mayor of Casterbridge, dynamically generated from wiki entries.

memes.label: Constable Stubberd
memes.description: Casterbridge's constable — a representative of official authority whose encounters with the novel's central characters illuminate the gap between law and justice in Hardy's world.

memes.label: Donald Farfrae
memes.description: The young Scotchman whose arrival in Casterbridge changes everything — a man of method, charm, and modern competence whose steady rise mirrors and accelerates Henchard's catastrophic fall. Showcase page for person type.

memes.label: Dr. Chalkfield
memes.description: A councilman of Casterbridge who served as Mayor for the year preceding his death in Chapter 41. His absence from the magistrates' court in Chapter 34 allowed Henchard to preside in his place, and his death opened a vacancy on the council that led to Farfrae's nomination.

memes.label: Durnover
memes.description: The agricultural quarter of Casterbridge — home to the corn exchange and the economic life of the town, representing the rural foundation beneath urban commerce.

memes.label: Elizabeth-Jane Newson
memes.description: The quiet centre of the novel — the daughter sold at Weydon-Priors fair who returns to Casterbridge as a young woman, navigates uncertain parentage, and ultimately survives the wreckage of everyone around her through observation, restraint, and compassion.

memes.label: Elizabeth-Jane's Parentage
memes.description: The gradual revelation that Elizabeth-Jane is not Henchard's daughter but Newson's — a discovery that severs Henchard's last emotional attachment and contributes to his final isolation.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: A complete index of all plot events in Thomas Hardy's The Mayor of Casterbridge, dynamically generated from wiki entries.

memes.label: Farfrae Becomes Mayor
memes.description: Donald Farfrae's election as Mayor of Casterbridge — the formal completion of the power transfer from the old order (Henchard) to the new (Farfrae) in the town's political life.

memes.label: Farfrae's Arrival
memes.description: The arrival of Donald Farfrae in Casterbridge — a young Scotchman passing through on his way to America who is persuaded by Henchard to stay, becoming first his manager and then his rival.

memes.label: Fate and Determinism
memes.description: The pervasive Hardy-esque fatalism in The Mayor of Casterbridge — the sense that characters are driven by forces beyond their control, often conflated with their own natures.

memes.label: Gender and Power
memes.description: The gendered distribution of power in The Mayor of Casterbridge — how Victorian gender roles constrain female characters and how masculine pride becomes a destructive force.

memes.label: Guilt and Redemption
memes.description: The moral economy of The Mayor of Casterbridge — whether guilt can be expiated through suffering, and whether Hardy's Naturalist universe permits anything resembling redemption.

memes.label: Henchard as Tragic Hero
memes.description: A synthetic analysis of Michael Henchard as tragic hero — tracing his relationship to Aristotle's hamartia, Shakespearean tragic figures, and Hardy's Naturalist revision of the tragic tradition.

memes.label: Henchard's Abstinence Vow
memes.description: Henchard's solemn vow to abstain from alcohol for twenty-one years — the act of will that enables his rise to mayor and whose eventual breaking precipitates his final decline.

memes.label: Henchard's Bankruptcy
memes.description: Henchard's financial collapse — the moment his commercial empire crumbles under the weight of bad speculation and Farfrae's competition, stripping him of the economic foundation of his social position.

memes.label: Henchard's Death
memes.description: The final, devastating event of the novel — Michael Henchard dies alone in a cottage outside Budmouth, leaving behind a will of absolute self-negation that is one of Hardy's most powerful passages. Showcase page for event type.

memes.label: Henchard's Reconciliation Attempt
memes.description: Henchard's final attempt to reconcile with Elizabeth-Jane at her wedding to Farfrae — an effort that, like all his attempts at reparation, arrives too late and in the wrong form.

memes.label: High-Place Hall
memes.description: Lucetta Templeman's residence in Casterbridge — a grand house whose elevated position and visible windows make it both a symbol of social ambition and a site of dangerous exposure.

memes.label: Joshua Jopp
memes.description: Henchard's former manager — a man of questionable competence and loyalty whose resentment at being passed over becomes a key plot catalyst when he delivers Lucetta's letters.

memes.label: Lucetta Templeman
memes.description: The woman from Henchard's past whose need for respectability and whose vulnerable social position make her the novel's most exposed character — destroyed by the collision of private passion and public judgment.

memes.label: Lucetta's Death
memes.description: The death of Lucetta Templeman (Mrs. Farfrae) following the trauma of the skimmity-ride — the novel's most direct presentation of the lethal power of public shame.

memes.label: Marriage and Domestic Life
memes.description: The institution of marriage as both plot mechanism and thematic lens in The Mayor of Casterbridge — examined through Henchard's disastrous first marriage, his strategic remarriage, and the domestic arrangements that structure the novel's social world.

memes.label: Mayor of Casterbridge
memes.description: The civic office of Mayor in Casterbridge — a position of supreme local authority that Michael Henchard achieves through force of will and loses through force of character, and which Donald Farfrae inherits as the embodiment of modern commercial competence.

memes.label: Mellstock
memes.description: A rural village near Casterbridge (Hardy's Wessex name for Stinsford) — representing the traditional agricultural communities from which characters come and to which some return.

memes.label: Michael Henchard
memes.description: The tragic protagonist of The Mayor of Casterbridge — a hay-trusser who rises to become mayor through force of will, and whose character flaws (pride, temper, jealousy) ensure his catastrophic downfall. Showcase page for person type.

memes.label: Mixen Lane
memes.description: The disreputable back-street of Casterbridge — home to the town's outcasts and criminals, and the source of the skimmity-ride that destroys Lucetta.

memes.label: Mother Cuxsom
memes.description: An elderly Casterbridge woman who tends to Susan Henchard in her final illness — representative of the informal networks of female care that sustain community life.

memes.label: Mr. Buzzford
memes.description: A general dealer of Casterbridge and one of the town's chorus of observers — a member of the fraternity of townspeople (with Solomon Longways and Christopher Coney) who witness and comment on the novel's central events.

memes.label: Mr. Grower
memes.description: A prominent burgess and creditor of Casterbridge, witness to Michael Henchard's marriage, and a figure in the town's council affairs — representing the established commercial class that observes and participates in Henchard's downfall.

memes.label: Mr. Joy
memes.description: A Casterbridge auctioneer and town functionary — representative of the professional class whose commercial activities form the background texture of the market town's economic life.

memes.label: Mrs. Blowbody
memes.description: A lady of Casterbridge who appears among the crowd during the royal visit — one of the townswomen who witness and comment on the public drama of Henchard's downfall.

memes.label: Mrs. Stannidge
memes.description: The landlady of the King's Arms, Casterbridge's principal inn, and wife of host Stannidge — a figure who observes the town's commercial and social life from behind the bar.

memes.label: Nance Mockridge
memes.description: A Casterbridge townswoman whose sharp observations and gossip provide a choral commentary on the novel's events from the perspective of ordinary townspeople.

memes.label: Nature and Landscape
memes.description: "Hardy's use of the natural world — weather, landscape, seasonal cycles — as more than backdrop: an active force that mirrors, foreshadows, and sometimes determines human action in The Mayor of Casterbridge."

memes.label: Newson's Return
memes.description: Richard Newson's unexpected return to Casterbridge — the sailor presumed dead who comes to claim his daughter Elizabeth-Jane, triggering Henchard's final crisis of abandonment.

memes.label: Peter's Finger
memes.description: The inn on Mixen Lane described as "the church of Mixen Lane" — a disreputable tavern that serves as the headquarters of Casterbridge's underworld and the staging-ground for the skimmity-ride that destroys Lucetta Templeman.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: A complete index of all locations and settings in Thomas Hardy's The Mayor of Casterbridge, dynamically generated from wiki entries.

memes.label: Power and Authority
memes.description: The forms and functions of power in Casterbridge — political (the mayor's office), economic (the corn trade), and personal (the will to dominate others), and how each form of authority proves unstable in Hardy's world.

memes.label: Pride and Humiliation
memes.description: Michael Henchard's defining tragic flaw — an intense, defensive pride that drives his rise to mayor and catalyzes his catastrophic downfall through repeated acts of self-sabotage.

memes.label: Pride and Humiliation
memes.description: The interplay of pride and humiliation as a central psychological dynamic in The Mayor of Casterbridge, driving Henchard's tragedy and shaping the novel's moral vision.

memes.label: Regret and Suffering
memes.description: The emotional core of The Mayor of Casterbridge — Henchard's overwhelming regret for his past actions and the suffering that radiates outward to everyone connected to him.

memes.label: Reputation and Social Standing
memes.description: The currency of public opinion in provincial Casterbridge — how reputation governs behaviour, enables or constrains action, and functions as a form of social control in Hardy's novel.

memes.label: Richard Newson
memes.description: The sailor who buys Susan at Weydon-Priors fair for five guineas, gives her a decent life, and returns after her death to claim his daughter Elizabeth-Jane — triggering the novel's final crisis.

memes.label: Rural Economics
memes.description: The agricultural and commercial economy of Hardy's Wessex as the material foundation of The Mayor of Casterbridge — the corn trade, rural labor, and market-town dynamics that shape every character's possibilities.

memes.label: Solomon Longways
memes.description: A Casterbridge resident who serves as an informal town historian and source of local knowledge — part of the network of observers through whom Hardy registers the community's collective response to events.

memes.label: Susan Henchard
memes.description: Michael Henchard's wife — sold at Weydon-Priors fair, who returns eighteen years later as a widow seeking security, and whose quiet endurance embodies the novel's critique of women's position in Victorian marriage.

memes.label: Susan's Return
memes.description: Susan Henchard's return to Casterbridge after Newson's presumed death at sea — the event that brings the past crashing into Henchard's present and sets the novel's domestic drama in motion.

memes.label: Temper and Impulse
memes.description: Michael Henchard's impulsive temperament — the "hot temper" that drives his most consequential decisions, from the opening wife-auction to his final self-destructive acts.

memes.label: The Bridge
memes.description: The bridge over the river at Casterbridge — a liminal space associated with Henchard's lowest moments and his contemplation of suicide.

memes.label: The Carrier's Rest
memes.description: An inn in Casterbridge serving rural carriers and travellers — a node in the transport network connecting the market town to its agricultural hinterland.

memes.label: The Church
memes.description: The parish church of Casterbridge — a centre of religious and social life, where baptisms, marriages, and funerals mark the rites of passage that structure the novel's events.

memes.label: The Furmity-Woman
memes.description: The itinerant furmity-seller at Weydon-Priors fair in whose tent Henchard sells his wife — and who reappears in Casterbridge two decades later to expose his past.

memes.label: The Furmity-Woman's Revelation
memes.description: The furmity-woman from Weydon-Priors fair reappears in Casterbridge, arrested for a petty crime, and publicly reveals Henchard's past — the wife-auction — to the magistrates' court.

memes.label: The King's Arms
memes.description: The chief hotel of Casterbridge — a spacious and respectable establishment that serves as the stage for Henchard's commercial triumphs and social humiliations, and the principal setting for the town's public life.

memes.label: The Market-Place
memes.description: The central public square of Casterbridge — where commerce, social life, and public spectacle converge, and where Henchard's authority is most visibly enacted and challenged.

memes.label: The Mayor of Casterbridge
memes.description: Overview of Thomas Hardy's 1886 novel The Mayor of Casterbridge — its plot, characters, themes, and critical significance within the Wessex canon.

memes.label: The Ring
memes.description: The ancient Roman amphitheatre at the edge of Casterbridge — a site of secret meetings, public spectacle, and symbolic encounters throughout the novel.

memes.label: The Rivalry
memes.description: The escalating commercial and personal rivalry between Michael Henchard and Donald Farfrae — a competition that transforms from professional respect into obsessive enmity and drives Henchard to financial and psychological ruin.

memes.label: The River
memes.description: The river that flows through Casterbridge — a natural boundary and a symbolic presence associated with death, cleansing, and the indifferent force of nature.

memes.label: The Royal Visit
memes.description: A royal personage visits Casterbridge — an event that should showcase Henchard's mayoral authority but instead becomes a public stage for his humiliation when Farfrae is chosen to organize the reception.

memes.label: The Skimmity-Ride
memes.description: The brutal public shaming ritual orchestrated by the denizens of Mixen Lane — a skimmity-ride featuring effigies of Henchard and Lucetta that exposes their past relationship and destroys Lucetta.

memes.label: The Three Mariners
memes.description: The modest Casterbridge inn where Farfrae first lodges upon arriving in town — a contrast to the grander King's Arms and a site of pivotal encounters.

memes.label: The Wife-Auction
memes.description: The originating event of The Mayor of Casterbridge — a young hay-trusser, drunk and desperate, auctions his wife and child for five guineas at Weydon-Priors fair, setting in motion the novel's entire tragic arc. Showcase page for event type.

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: A leaderboard of the most-cited pages in the The Mayor of Casterbridge wiki, showing which characters, places, events, and concepts are most central to the novel's critical and editorial apparatus.

memes.label: The Town Hall
memes.description: The civic building of Casterbridge, situated below High-Place Hall and adjacent to the Market House — a site of public assembly, council proceedings, and the town's official life.

memes.label: Tradition and Change
memes.description: The tension between traditional rural ways and modern commercial methods in The Mayor of Casterbridge — embodied in the Henchard-Farfrae rivalry and the transformation of Wessex agriculture.

memes.label: Weatherbury
memes.description: A rural village in Casterbridge's orbit — part of the network of agricultural communities that comprise Hardy's Wessex, mentioned alongside Mellstock as an origin point for the town's population.

memes.label: Weydon-Priors
memes.description: The village fairground where the novel's originating event occurs — Henchard's drunken sale of his wife Susan to the sailor Newson.

memes.label: Women in Casterbridge
memes.description: A cross-cutting analysis of the three central female characters — Susan Henchard, Elizabeth-Jane Newson, and Lucetta Templeman — as a spectrum of female agency, vulnerability, and survival in Victorian provincial society.
